title: The Algorithm Knows You Better
synopsis: Something happened this year that I think deserves more attention than it received, though it received quite a lot. Facebook's advertising platform...
preview: Something happened this year that I think deserves more attention than it received, though it received quite a lot. Facebook's advertising platform...
content:
Something happened this year that I think deserves more attention than it received, though it received quite a lot.

Facebook's advertising platform now allows targeting at a level of psychological specificity that would have sounded like science fiction a decade ago. You can target people who are likely to be feeling insecure. People who have recently moved. People whose purchasing behaviour suggests a relationship breakdown. People who are probably pregnant before they have told anyone. The platform knows, because the data knows, because the patterns in human behaviour are legible to anyone with enough data and sufficient computational power to read them.

I have spent enough time in product design to understand what this means technically. The models are not magic. They are correlations, immensely sophisticated correlations, trained on the behavioural residue of two billion people. Every click, every pause, every scroll that lingers a moment too long, these are data points, and in sufficient quantity, data points become a portrait. Not a perfect portrait. But a useful one. Useful enough to sell things. Useful enough to shift behaviour. Useful enough to matter.

What interests me is not the technology itself but the asymmetry it creates.

Two billion people are legible to a handful of platforms. Their preferences, their vulnerabilities, their habits, their fears, all of it readable, all of it actionable. The platforms, meanwhile, are opaque. Opaque to the users who feed them data. Opaque to the governments that nominally regulate them. Opaque, increasingly, to the engineers who build them, as the models grow complex enough that their specific reasoning becomes difficult to trace.

This is a new form of power. I want to be precise about that claim because power is a word that gets thrown around loosely. What I mean is this: the ability to model human behaviour at scale is the ability to influence human behaviour at scale. And the entities that possess this ability are not governments, not elected bodies, not institutions with any form of democratic accountability. They are corporations, headquartered in California, accountable to shareholders, optimising for engagement.

The user is not the customer. This is well understood. The user is the product. But even this framing undersells what is happening. The user is the raw material. The product is prediction, the ability to predict what a specific person will do, want, feel, or buy, with sufficient accuracy to make that prediction commercially valuable. The customer is whoever will pay for access to that predictive power. Advertisers, obviously. But the same predictive infrastructure is available to anyone with a budget: political campaigns, foreign governments, domestic intelligence agencies, anyone who benefits from knowing what people think and being able to influence what they think next.

I keep returning to the question of what this means for a country like Australia.

We have no domestic platforms of any significance. No Australian search engine. No Australian social network. No Australian cloud infrastructure at the scale that matters. The digital infrastructure through which Australian public life increasingly operates is entirely foreign-owned, primarily American, and subject to the laws and commercial incentives of a jurisdiction that is not ours.

When Australians discuss politics on Facebook, they do so inside a system whose algorithmic priorities are set in Menlo Park. When they search for information on Google, the ranking of results is determined by engineers in Mountain View. When they store their data in the cloud, it resides on servers owned by Amazon or Microsoft, subject to American law, accessible under American legal frameworks that Australian citizens have no ability to influence.

This is a form of dependence that we do not have a good vocabulary for. It is not colonialism in the traditional sense. No one is being coerced. The platforms are genuinely useful. People choose to use them because they are good products, well-designed, and they provide real value. The dependence is voluntary, which makes it harder to name and harder to resist.

But voluntary dependence on systems you do not control and cannot influence is still dependence. And dependence on systems that can model your behaviour, predict your choices, and shape your information environment is a particular kind of dependence that I think we should be more concerned about than we are.

The regulatory response, such as it is, has been inadequate. This is not a specifically Australian failure. No government anywhere has figured out how to regulate these platforms effectively. The technology moves faster than the regulatory process. The platforms have more technical expertise than the agencies meant to oversee them. The lobbying budgets are substantial. The revolving door between industry and government turns smoothly.

But the Australian situation has a specific dimension that makes it worse. We are not merely failing to regulate effectively. We are attempting to regulate systems that we have no domestic alternative to. When the European Union pushes back against American platforms, it does so from a position of some strength, a large market, some domestic capability, institutional frameworks with teeth. When Australia pushes back, it does so as a small market with no alternatives, no domestic capability, and limited leverage.

The Assistance and Access Act, passed this year, illustrates the confusion perfectly. The government wants the ability to access encrypted communications for law enforcement purposes. Set aside the merits of that goal. The implementation requires Australian law to reach into systems designed, built, and operated by foreign companies under foreign law. The assumption that this is possible, that Australian legislation can meaningfully compel behaviour from entities whose infrastructure, expertise, and legal domicile are all elsewhere, reveals a gap between the government's understanding of how these systems work and how they actually work.

We are legislating control over infrastructure we do not own. The exercise amounts to theatre.

Whether a solution is available at this point, I honestly cannot say. The decisions that could have established meaningful digital sovereignty were not made decades ago, when domestic technology industries might have been cultivated. We integrated enthusiastically into global digital systems because they were good and they were free and nobody thought very hard about what the integration would cost.

What I do know is that the asymmetry is growing. The platforms know more about Australians than any Australian institution knows about Australians. They can model our behaviour, predict our choices, shape our information environment. They do this not out of malice but out of commercial logic, which is in some ways more concerning, because commercial logic does not stop when the consequences become problematic. It optimises regardless.

From where I sit, in a country that produces no digital infrastructure of consequence and consumes an enormous amount of it, the trajectory is clear. We will become more legible to systems we do not control. Our behaviour will become more predictable. Our information environment will become more shaped. And we will have less ability to influence any of it than we do today, which is already very little.

The algorithm knows you better than you know yourself. That is the claim, and it is increasingly supported by the evidence. The question nobody in Canberra seems to be asking is what it means to be a democracy when the most powerful tools for understanding and influencing citizens are controlled by entities that the democratic process cannot touch.

I do not have an answer. But I think the question is worth stating clearly, because the longer we avoid it, the fewer options we will have when we finally confront it.
